topic: Traits - Borrow and BorrowMut for Collections

turns.content: why does this even work this way? I'm trying to add a custom type to my HashMap with String keys, but the compiler is screaming about Hash+Eq+Ord requirements when I try to use Borrow. It feels like Rust is forcing me to implement too many traits just to store something in a collection.

That frustration is totally understandable because it's not immediately obvious that HashMap enforces those specific trait bounds internally. The core issue is that HashMap::get uses the Borrow trait contract which requires the borrowed form to have Hash+Eq+Ord, while AsRef just requires Deref and doesn't guarantee those traits on the underlying type itself. So when you borrow a custom struct with &self, Rust needs to ensure that exact type can be hashed and compared for the map lookup to work correctly.
So I don't need to implement Borrow myself since it's auto-derived? But then why does HashMap::get require those specific bounds on the borrowed value?

You're right that std::borrow::Borrow is implemented automatically for most structs via derive, but the real constraint comes from how HashMap implements its internal lookup. When you call HashMap::get with a &str key, the map needs to hash and compare that exact borrowed type against stored keys. Since HashMap stores owned values internally, it requires the borrowed version of your custom type to satisfy Hash+Eq+Ord so it can safely perform those operations during iteration or retrieval.
Ah okay, so I need to explicitly derive both traits for my struct? But what if I only want to use AsRef and not actually store the full type in the map?

Exactly! If you only care about looking up by string slices without storing complex data tied to those keys, using a wrapper with AsRef is cleaner. You can define a simple struct that implements Deref to String or other types, letting HashMap::get work through AsRef's trait bounds which are much lighter than the full Borrow contract requiring Hash+Eq+Ord.

That makes sense. But I'm still confused about the difference between Borrow and BorrowMut in this context since both seem to involve borrowing. When would I actually need BorrowMut instead of just Borrow?

BorrowMut is specifically needed when you want to modify the stored value through the map, whereas Borrow only allows reading. If your custom type has mutable fields that need updating via HashMap::get_mut, then BorrowMut becomes necessary because it grants the ability to call methods that take &mut self rather than just &self.
I see. So for my game logic where I'm storing entity states in a map and occasionally changing their properties, should I use Borrow or BorrowMut?

Since you're modifying entity states in your HashMap, you'll need BorrowMut whenever retrieving an entry to update its mutable fields. However, if most of your operations are just reading the state before applying changes, using Borrow for lookups and then borrowing mut only during the actual modification phase is the most efficient pattern.
Got it. But what if I want to avoid the overhead of implementing all those traits? Is there a way to make HashMap work with my custom type without deriving everything?

The best approach is to use a wrapper struct that implements Deref to your actual data type, letting AsRef handle the reference semantics. This way you only need to derive Deref and let the compiler infer the necessary bounds for HashMap operations, avoiding manual trait implementation overhead while still satisfying the map's requirements.
Wait, I think I misunderstood something earlier. When I tried using a custom struct with Borrow, did the error come from the borrowed form not having Hash+Eq+Ord or from the type itself?

The error specifically came from your custom struct's borrowed form lacking those traits. Even though std::borrow::Borrow is auto-derived, HashMap requires that exact type to satisfy Hash+Eq+Ord because it uses those methods during its internal operations like hashing and equality comparison for safe lookups.
Okay so I should just derive both traits explicitly? Is there any performance benefit to using AsRef instead of the full Borrow approach?

AsRef is lighter since it only requires Deref, but if you need the borrowed form to be hashable and comparable for HashMap operations, you still need those specific bounds regardless. The real advantage comes when your type doesn't require hashing at all or when you're doing read-only lookups where Borrow isn't strictly necessary.
That's helpful. One last thing though - if I'm storing multiple copies of the same entity data in my map, does using Borrow affect memory usage compared to owning the full type?

Actually, since HashMap stores owned values internally and you're borrowing from those stored values, there's no extra memory overhead beyond what the original storage already requires. The borrow itself is just a reference without additional allocation, so your memory footprint stays consistent whether you use Borrow or AsRef approaches.
